  • Internals and Porting Notes
    • Available internals pages
  • diCal2 Native Parity Notes
    • Why these changes were necessary
    • What changed
      • 1. Shared upstream/native result normalization
      • 2. VCF reader now preserves upstream-style physical-locus metadata
      • 3. Grouped-locus counting now uses physical blocks, not just segregating columns
      • 4. Refined intervals now materialize interval-specific epochs
      • 5. Meta-start generation was tightened toward upstream semantics
      • 6. Gallery rerun used a targeted diCal2-only render path
      • 7. Meta-start replay tracing is now available on the native path
      • 8. Java nextLong() and coordinatewise shuffle semantics were still off
      • 9. VCF-backed native configs needed to be compacted after filtering
      • 10. The remaining IM mismatch is concentrated in cross-pop PCL cases
    • What improved
      • 11. Structured no-growth native runs needed ODECore, not EigenCore
    • What still does not match
    • Next steps
  • PSMC Internals
    • Why this page exists

    • Core model

    • What stayed out of the main docs
  • SMC++ Parity Closure Notes
    • Scope
    • Final state
    • What was actually wrong
      • 1. Native one-pop preprocessing was not phase-aligned with upstream
      • 2. Native one-pop HMM was leaving the upstream compressed-row ceremony
    • How the issue was found
    • The fixes that closed parity
      • Upstream preprocessing offset in native one-pop preprocessing
      • Stay on the compressed run-length stream in the one-pop HMM
    • Earlier fixes that mattered
      • One-pop Watterson estimator
      • Monomorphic one-pop emission handling
      • One-pop transition details
      • Prefit / initialization parity
      • Scale-step optimizer semantics
    • What did not close parity by itself
    • Practical lesson
    • Code pointers

        • Settings.upstream_cache_dir
        • Settings.upstream_runtime_overrides
        • Settings.use_gpu()
smckit
  • Start Here
  • View page source

smckit: A home for SMC algorithms.

smckit keeps classic Sequentially Markovian Coalescent methods usable now while native Python implementations mature method by method.

The core contract is simple: implementation="upstream" runs the original tool when ready, implementation="native" runs the in-repo port, and implementation="auto" prefers upstream fidelity. Use smckit.upstream.status() to see exactly which runtimes and bootstraps are ready on your machine.

Unified SmcData workflow Dual implementation API Public upstream runners Numba-native ports Vendored upstream references 
import smckit

data = smckit.io.read_psmcfa("sample.psmcfa")
data = smckit.tl.psmc(
    data,
    pattern="4+5*3+4",
    n_iterations=25,
    implementation="native",
)
smckit.pl.demographic_history(data)
Start with a quickstart if you want working code, use the method guide if you are choosing between tools, and only dive into internals if you are porting or validating an algorithm.

Start Here

Understand the philosophy Why smckit exists, how upstream and native implementations coexist, and how to think about the project. Choose a method Pick the right SMC tool by sample type, inference target, and current implementation status. See the tool API Review the public inference functions and the shared implementation= selector. Inspect parity details Read the fixture-by-fixture agreement notes behind the current status matrix.

Implementation Status

smckit-section-intro

smckit treats implementation provenance as part of the public interface. The matrix below is for users: it tells you which path exists today and whether native parity is strong enough to be considered default-eligible in docs. Runtime requirements still matter: diCal2 needs a working Java runtime, ASMC needs its C++/Boost build stack, and SMC++ still depends on a controlled side Python environment.

smckit-method-note

Check Gallery for the visual side-by-side evidence behind these status marks, especially where a method is marked unavailable, unstable, or not yet default-eligible.

Method

Upstream

Native

Native default eligible

Tracked agreement

Notes

PSMC

✓

✓

✓

0.9999223 lambda corr

Public upstream bridge now runs the vendored binary; native port remains stable.

ASMC

✓

✓

✓

0.9984 MAP agreement

Public upstream path uses the documented ASMC executable outputs.

MSMC2

✓

✓

✓

>= 0.999999865 lambda corr

Public upstream bridge runs the vendored MSMC2 CLI.

MSMC-IM

✓

✓

✓

strict payload match on 4-case upstream-backed oracle matrix

Public upstream bridge runs the vendored Python fitter; native and upstream now share the same public payload contract and are interchangeable on the enforced Yoruba/French oracle matrix.

eSMC2

✓

✓

✗

<= 0.3605% max tracked Xi rel err

Both paths exist; tracked fit parity is enforced for fixed-rho, rho-redo, beta, sigma, beta+sigma, and grouped pop_vect=[3,3], with upstream still preferred outside that matrix.

SMC++

✓

✓

✓

>= 0.999125 tracked one-pop log-Ne corr

Public upstream path exists; the native one-pop path now clears the strict small control and larger tracked .smc parity fixtures, with fixed-model E-step statistics matching upstream on the same matrix.

diCal2

✓

✓

✗

README exp search matches best params; fixed-point dloglik=7.45e-4. README IM search matches best params; fixed-point dloglik=3.15e-2.

Public upstream Java bridge parses the EM-path stdout into structured results. Native README exp and IM searches now reach the upstream best-fit parameter vectors; the remaining gap is the native fixed-point likelihood value at those shared points.

SSM

n/a

✓

✗

—

Novel in-repo extension framework rather than an upstream compatibility target.
smckit-method-note

Detailed fixture notes, caveats, and remaining parity gaps live on Parity and Oracle Status.

smckit-method-note

Methods marked ✗ in Native default eligible still run natively, but the package emits a warning telling users to prefer upstream plus the relevant smckit[…] install hint when that path matters.

Choose A Method At A Glance

smckit-section-intro

Use this table when you already know the rough shape of your data or question. The status matrix above answers what is runnable today; this guide answers which method family to click into next.

smckit-method-note

Starting from a VCF (or similar variant data) and metadata? Use this quick decision path first, then use the table to pick the concrete method page.

Fast Decision Path (VCF + Metadata)

  1. Do you already have model files (`.param` / `.demo` / `.config`)?

    • Yes -> diCal2

    • No -> continue

  2. Are your genomes mostly unphased and do you have many samples?

    • Yes -> SMC++ (best fit for many unphased genomes)

    • No -> continue

  3. Do you have one diploid genome (or one pairwise sequence)?

    • Yes -> PSMC

    • If dormancy/selfing is biologically important -> eSMC2

    • No -> continue

  4. Do you have phased haplotypes (typically 2-8 haplotypes)?

    • Yes -> MSMC2

    • Need per-site pairwise TMRCA/local ancestry instead of one curve? -> ASMC

    • Need two-population migration curves after MSMC/MSMC2? -> MSMC-IM

Quick Feature-to-Tool Table

Starting point

Phased?

Typical sample shape

Need mu / r upfront?

Best first tool

Next step

VCF + demographic model files

optional

structured-model workflows

usually yes

diCal2

tune model files and rerun fits

VCF-like data, many genomes

mostly unphased

many individuals

yes

SMC++

compare native/upstream provenance

single diploid genome

n/a

one genome

yes

PSMC

eSMC2 for dormancy/selfing

phased haplotypes

yes

2-8 haplotypes

yes

MSMC2

MSMC-IM for migration summaries

phased haplotypes + local ancestry target

yes

pairwise decoding

yes

ASMC

inspect per-site TMRCA tracks

Method

Best for

Input

Output focus

Current fit

PSMC

one diploid genome

.psmcfa

N_e(t)

strong native baseline and the standard entry point for single-genome history inference

eSMC2

one diploid genome with dormancy or selfing

.psmcfa or pairwise sequence input

N_e(t), beta, sigma

native and upstream paths exist; tracked parity now covers the public .psmcfa and multihetsep input families, with the R-backed upstream bridge still the fidelity baseline outside that matrix

MSMC2

two to eight phased haplotypes

.multihetsep

coalescence rates and N_e(t)

strong fixed-fixture validation with an implementation surface that is still growing

MSMC-IM

two-population MSMC or MSMC2 follow-up analysis

.combined.msmc2.final.txt

N1(t), N2(t), m(t), M(t)

tracked 4-case upstream-backed oracle matrix now shows interchangeable native/upstream payloads on the enforced Yoruba/French fixtures

SMC++

many unphased genomes

.smc or .smc.gz

N_e(t)

tracked one-pop native/upstream parity now holds on both the strict small control and bundled larger .smc fixture

ASMC

per-site pairwise ancestry or TMRCA on phased haplotypes

hap/map/samples plus decoding quantities

TMRCA along the genome

strong native path for local ancestry-style questions rather than one population-size curve

diCal2

explicit structured demographic model files

.param, .demo, .config, sequences

sizes, growth, and migration parameters

best fit when the demographic specification already exists outside smckit, with upstream still the fidelity baseline while native exp oracle-point and explicit-start agreement tighten

SSM Framework / PSMC-SSM

method development, HMM inspection, and gradient fitting

psmcfa-style observations

differentiable PSMC and explicit state-space models

native-only experimental framework rather than an upstream compatibility target

Quickstarts

PSMCOne diploid genome, native port, classic entry point. ASMCPairwise TMRCA decoding for phased haplotypes. MSMC2Multiple haplotypes, validated fixtures, still growing. MSMC-IMTwo-population migration fitting from MSMC output. eSMC2Dormancy and selfing on top of pairwise SMC. SMC++Many unphased genomes with upstream/native provenance. diCal2Structured demography from native model files.

Benchmarks

On NA12878 chr22 (10 EM iterations):

Backend

E-step

Full run

vs C

Numba JIT

0.52s

5.4s

0.85x (17% faster)

C PSMC (reference)

–

6.3s

1.0x

NumPy (pure Python)

6.1s

~65s

10.2x
